speaker: interviewee
text: This is my paternal grandparents. Her name was [? Dvera, ?] and his was [? Shleme ?] Shyer. They were very religious people. I didn't know them too much. I don't remember them at all. My grandfather died in Europe. My mother-- my grandmother died here in [INAUDIBLE]. That's all I know about them. Before I came here. The first one is show Szloma, and I don't know how old is he. I really don't know. Then next to him is my mother, Gitl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you going from left to right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then it's my father, Symcha. Then it's me. And that's my-- my father's mother, Jachna. And in the end is Abraham. And my mother and Abraham died here, in Boston. That's all I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, my mother was in the 80's, and my brother was '93. That's all I know. It's-- The picture was taken on our wedding day. It's my husband. His name is [? Sholen. ?] And this is me. It was done in Ulm, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '49-- January 4th, '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you married in a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think so. I really am not sure. I know it was done very traditional. And the rabbi was ultra orthodox. But I guess to the pictures, my husband had to take off his, uh, yarmulke. So the rest, I don't-- I don't remember it. But it was done in Ulm by orthodox rabbi. This is my husband's parents, and my husband is a baby. Her name was Reisha [INAUDIBLE]. And that's my husband, [? Scholen, ?] the baby. And that's his father. His name is Leibel. That's all I know about them. And this was, um-- the pictures, he found them here, in the United States, in New York. They had their family there, so the pictures are all-- family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this picture was taken in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the pictures were taken in Europe, but they found them in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, I guess [INAUDIBLE]. Let's say [? Homsk. ?] That's the biggest city. [? Homsk. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: In approximately what year was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gee, it was taken probably in-- in-- he was born 1918, so this was taken probably 1919 or 1918.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did his parents do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. 1919, this picture was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because-- That's my, uh-- my husband's grandfather, sitting down. The next to him is my husband. Next to my husband is his cousin, which were killed in the partisans. And that's his grandmother. They only know his cousin's name was Yankel. And two names, I don't recall on the minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Europe, probably in 1940-- no, in 1935, probably. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah, that's the only I can think in 1935, to this. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because he was, uh-- when the Russians came in, he was 18 years old there. So it must be then [INAUDIBLE], something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first one is Louise. And she is the bride. Next to her is my son, Larry. They used to call him [? Leibeler. ?] And then comes Janet and Sandy. They're sisters of the groom. And my mother, who was given the biggest honor-- she's sitting down, Gitl. She was-- by the time, she was in year 80's. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's it. And the picture was taken in Montreal.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just was saying, in '80-- '75.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did we mention that already?

speaker: interviewee
text: The wedding was in '75.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it. It's Shane's bar mitzvah, my first grandson, which I'm very proud of him. And he's a wonderful kid-- traditional, everything a grandmother could wish for.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the next one-- you're going to film, too, the other picture? This is Larry putting on the tallith, and Shane. I really adore this boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the firstborn, and the nicest kid growing up in the [INAUDIBLE], when it was here, he never changed his attitude. A good son, a good father, and a good husband. And I'm very proud of him. He went to schools. He-- I want to say once that he came up in a holiday, and he was wearing jeans that were torn-- in [INAUDIBLE] his hair. My husband made him to go to the bathroom and take everything over and put on everything clean and sit down near the table and having the seder. And he did it without saying a word. That's all I have to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my baby, Sandy. And next to her is her husband, Paul, which is a wonderful guy. And then it's me, and Sandy's three kids. Gabriel, near me, is named after my mother, Gitl, and that's why he's so special to me. And then comes Emily and Zachary. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Wonderful three kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken-- uh, I guess last year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Must be. Yeah, in 1995. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something on the-- I guess, the Caribbean coast.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first one is the old lady, me, which they dragged me all over, wherever they go. And then comes Sandy. And after Sandy, it's Janet. Janet was my favorite, I-- I admit. And I'm ashamed to say it, but she was my favorite all the years. Easygoing, very easygoing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in-- in a cruise ship to the Caribbean. I don't know exactly where it was. Because it's so many pictures I have. But we went on a cruise, all as a family. Janet, Sandy, the kids, the husband, and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: When? It was-- everything-- this was two years ago, I would say. Because last year, I was in Alaska. Two years ago. It's-- it's '90--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sandy, Larry, and Janet. And this was taken in front of our house. And this picture must be taken in '92 or '93. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's my three favorite kids. [LAUGHS] I haven't got no more. That's it. This was done on Janet's wedding and here's my kids with their spouses. And my brother was still alive. And it's my brother here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Aby. ?] That's all I had. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: The wedding was in-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] '85. '85? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It must be in '85.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that here in Brooklyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was in Brooklyn. Temple [INAUDIBLE]. That's it. It's my husband Shane and Gabriel. The best grandfather I ever met, to devote his life to grandchildren as he did, loving, caring and giving. [SOBS] He always said to them, 'If you're going to go to college, I'll pay for everything. You don't have to worry about anything.' That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in-- well, it's been about nine years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Right here, probably, because he didn't go no place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nine years ago, so what year? Uh, '87, something like this. And he died-- I want to say this. He always dreamed to go to Shane's bar mitzvah. He died just two months before, in five minutes of a heart attack. [SOBS] And two-- two months after, we made a beautiful bar bar mitzvah. Everybody was dancing and singing, whatever it was. And we said, he wanted this way. He probably wanted it, because he was very fond of the-- them. He's a father who had all the [INAUDIBLE], but anybody can give. Never a minute trouble from three kids. That's all I can say. Lucky man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
